planting.note: Plant rooted cuttings in spring in moist, moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral, well-drained to moderately moist soil in full sun to part shade; Rangoon creeper is one of the most spectacular and fragrant tropical flowering vines — the flowers change color as they age, opening white, turning pink the next day, then deep red by the third day, so the vine always displays all three colors simultaneously, creating a tricolor effect; intensely sweet fragrance strongest in the evening.

directions: Plant in moist, moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral, well-drained to moderately moist soil in full sun to part shade; provide strong trellis or fence support; the vine is frost-tender but regenerates from the root in zone 9. Quisqualis indica (Rangoon creeper, Chinese honeysuckle, or drunken sailor) is one of the most flamboyant and unique tropical flowering vines available for zone 9–12 gardens — native to the tropical forests of South and Southeast Asia (India through the Philippines) and now one of the most widely grown tropical ornamental vines worldwide. The vine's most distinctive feature is its color-changing flowers: the 2-inch, long-tubed, star-shaped, fragrant flowers open pure white on the first day, turn soft pink on the second day, and deepen to brilliant scarlet-red by the third day — so the vine simultaneously carries clusters of white, pink, and red flowers at all times, creating a tricolor display unlike any other garden plant. The flowers have a powerful, sweet, jasmine-vanilla-like fragrance that intensifies in the evening, making them extraordinary for garden areas where evening fragrance is desired. The vine is a vigorous grower (reaching 20–30 feet) and blooms prolifically in warm weather. In zone 9, the vine dies back to the root in hard freezes but regenerates vigorously in spring — effectively functioning as a semi-perennial.
sources: University of Florida IFAS Extension — Quisqualis indica
Fairchild Tropical Botanic Garden — Tropical Vines

planting.note: Plant container stock in spring in very well-drained, lean, slightly alkaline to neutral, sandy soil in full sun to part shade; gout plant is a distinctive tropical ornamental shrub with a dramatically swollen, knobby, caudex-like base — resembling a twisted, gout-deformed joint — producing bright coral-red, small, terminal flower clusters above large, deeply lobed, tropical leaves; ALL parts are toxic; minimal water needed once established.

directions: Plant in very well-drained, lean, sandy soil in full sun to bright part shade; the swollen base is the ornamental feature — keep soil dry in winter to prevent rot; CAUTION: All parts are toxic. Jatropha podagrica (gout plant, Buddha belly plant, or tartogo) is a distinctive, slow-growing tropical ornamental shrub native to Central America — a member of the spurge family (Euphorbiaceae) and the Jatropha genus, widely known for its unusual and sculptural physical form. The most dramatic feature of the plant is its pachycaul (thickened-trunk) base: a grotesquely swollen, knobby, gnarled, bottle-shaped or bumpy stem base that looks like a massively enlarged, deformed joint — exactly resembling gout-afflicted knuckles (hence 'gout plant') or the pot-bellied figure of a Buddha (hence 'Buddha belly'). This caudex-like swollen base serves as a water reservoir, making the plant drought-tolerant. Above this remarkable structure, the plant produces large (8–12 inch across), deeply palmately lobed, bright-green leaves with a tropical appearance, and small clusters of vivid coral-red to vermillion-orange flowers on erect peduncles that attract hummingbirds and butterflies. Gout plant is a beloved collector's plant and pot plant in tropical horticulture for its unusual form, easy culture (very low water needs), and colorful flowers. CAUTION: All parts of the plant are toxic, similar to other Jatropha species.
sources: University of Florida IFAS Extension — Jatropha species
Fairchild Tropical Botanic Garden — Tropical Succulents

common_name: Thryallis (Shower of Gold, Rain of Gold)

planting.note: Plant container stock in spring in well-drained, lean to moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral, sandy to loamy soil in full sun; thryallis is a fast-growing, drought-tolerant Mexican native shrub producing masses of small, brilliant-yellow flowers on gracefully arching branches nearly year-round in warm climates — one of the most reliable and cheerful flowering shrubs for zone 9–12 Gulf Coast and tropical gardens.

directions: Plant in well-drained, lean to moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral, sandy to loamy soil in full sun; drought-tolerant once established; prune lightly after flowering to maintain compact shape. Galphimia gracilis (thryallis, shower of gold, or rain of gold) is a graceful, fast-growing, shrubby Mexican native in the Malpighiaceae family — one of the most reliably cheerful and floriferous flowering shrubs for zone 8–11 Gulf Coast and tropical gardens. The plant produces long, arching branches tipped with clusters of small (1/2-inch), five-petaled, brilliant golden-yellow flowers produced abundantly almost year-round in warm, frost-free climates — hence the evocative common names 'shower of gold' and 'rain of gold.' In zone 9–11, thryallis blooms nearly continuously from spring through fall, pausing only during the coldest months. The fine-textured foliage, graceful, fountain-like habit, and prolific golden flower production make it outstanding for mixed borders, foundation plantings, and as a flowering accent in drought-tolerant landscapes. It is also highly attractive to butterflies. Thryallis is more cold-hardy than many Mexican tropical shrubs, surviving brief dips to about 25°F once established in zone 8b; it dies back in cold winters but regrows from the root. A tremendously reliable and low-maintenance choice for hot, sunny, dry gardens throughout the Gulf South.
sources: University of Florida IFAS Extension — Galphimia gracilis
Texas A&M AgriLife Extension — Landscape Plants for Texas

common_name: Bay Rum Tree (West Indian Bay, Bay Laurel of the Caribbean)

planting.note: Plant container stock in spring in well-drained, lean to moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral, sandy to loamy soil in full sun; bay rum tree is a Caribbean native in the allspice family (Myrtaceae) — the aromatic, glossy leaves yield bay rum oil, the basis of the classic West Indian bay rum aftershave and hair tonic used throughout the Caribbean since the 19th century; the leaves can also be used as a culinary spice similar to bay laurel but with warmer, more complex, eugenol-clove notes.

directions: Plant in well-drained, lean to moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral, sandy to loamy soil in full sun; drought-tolerant once established; the leaves can be harvested year-round for culinary and aromatic use. Pimenta racemosa (bay rum tree or West Indian bay) is a small to medium-sized aromatic evergreen tree native to the Caribbean islands — particularly Puerto Rico, the Lesser Antilles, and the Virgin Islands — in the myrtle family (Myrtaceae), closely related to allspice (P. dioica). The glossy, elliptic, leathery leaves of P. racemosa are extraordinarily aromatic when crushed or steam-distilled — yielding 'bay oil' or 'bay rum oil,' a complex essential oil rich in eugenol (clove-scented), myrcene, and chavicol, with warm, spicy, clove-bay character. Bay rum oil is the defining ingredient in traditional West Indian bay rum — a classic men's aftershave, hair tonic, and skin care product used throughout the Caribbean since the mid-19th century and still produced in the Virgin Islands and Puerto Rico today. The same leaves can be used as a culinary herb: dried or fresh leaves add a warmer, more complex, clove-tinged bay flavor to Caribbean stews, soups, and marinades compared to Mediterranean bay laurel. Small, white, fragrant flowers are produced in panicles, followed by small, black, spice-flavored berries. An outstanding aromatic tree for zone 10–12 gardens that combines culinary, aromatic, and cultural heritage.
sources: University of Puerto Rico Agricultural Experiment Station — Pimenta racemosa
University of Florida IFAS Extension — Bay Rum Tree

planting.note: Plant container stock in spring in moist, humus-rich, well-drained, slightly acidic (pH 5.0–6.5), fertile, loamy soil in part shade; robusta coffee (C. canephora) is the second most important commercial coffee species after arabica (C. arabica) — more disease-resistant, more drought-tolerant, and higher in caffeine than arabica, but with a harsher, more bitter, earthier flavor; widely grown for espresso blends and instant coffee production; the natural understory habitat of the species makes it well-suited to part-shade garden positions.

directions: Plant in moist, humus-rich, well-drained, slightly acidic (pH 5.0–6.5), fertile, loamy soil in part shade; robusta coffee grows naturally as an understory shrub and performs best with afternoon shade in hot climates. Coffea canephora (robusta coffee or Congo coffee) is the second major commercial coffee species, accounting for approximately 40% of world coffee production — ranking second only to arabica (C. arabica) but distinctly different in origin, physiology, flavor, and agricultural use. C. canephora is native to the humid tropical forests of central and West Africa (particularly the Congo Basin region — hence 'Congo coffee'), where it grows as a spreading, multi-stemmed understory shrub. Robusta coffee has several significant differences from arabica: it is more disease-resistant (particularly to coffee leaf rust — Hemileia vastatrix — the devastating fungal disease that has devastated arabica crops worldwide); it is more drought-tolerant; it contains approximately twice the caffeine content of arabica (2–4% vs. 1.2–1.5%); and it can be grown at lower elevations and in hotter, more humid climates than arabica. These traits make robusta the preferred crop for hot, humid, lower-elevation tropical regions including Vietnam (the world's largest robusta producer), India, Ivory Coast, Uganda, and Indonesia. The flavor profile of robusta — stronger, more bitter, earthier, with woody and chocolate-cocoa notes — suits espresso blends (where it adds body and crema), instant coffee, and cold brew. In zone 10–12 gardens, both coffee species can be grown for home production and as ornamental shade-tolerant specimens.
sources: University of Florida IFAS Extension — Coffee Production
Kew Royal Botanic Gardens — Plants of the World Online

subcategory: tropical warm-season cover crop and ornamental

planting.note: Direct sow in spring after soil warms in well-drained, lean to moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; showy rattlebox is a fast-growing tropical annual legume cover crop used for nitrogen fixation and nematode suppression in warm-season rotation systems — particularly effective against root-knot nematodes (Meloidogyne species); IMPORTANT: toxic to horses, cattle, pigs, and poultry due to pyrrolizidine alkaloids in seeds and foliage — do NOT use as forage or in areas accessible to livestock.

directions: Broadcast or drill in warm season in full sun; incorporate before seed set; CAUTION: toxic to livestock — do not use as forage; effective nematode suppression cover crop. Crotalaria spectabilis (showy rattlebox or ornamental crotalaria) is a tropical annual legume native to Asia and Africa, naturalized across warm regions worldwide, and widely used in tropical and subtropical cover cropping systems for two important soil health benefits: nitrogen fixation (60–100 lbs N/acre) and nematode suppression. The plant's nematode-suppressing properties are its most important agronomic feature: C. spectabilis is one of the most effective non-chemical tools for managing root-knot nematodes (Meloidogyne species) — a devastating pest of vegetables, strawberries, and ornamentals in sandy soils of the Gulf South and tropical regions. The plant produces compounds that are toxic to nematode larvae, dramatically reducing nematode populations when grown for one season and incorporated into the soil. The plant is also ornamentally attractive: tall (3–5 feet), with bright-yellow, butterfly-like flower spikes and interesting inflated seed pods that rattle when dry (the genus name Crotalaria means 'rattle'). IMPORTANT CAUTION: The seeds and foliage contain pyrrolizidine alkaloids that are toxic to horses, cattle, pigs, and poultry — causing liver damage and death. Use only in areas inaccessible to livestock, or as a summer cover crop terminated before animals access the field.
sources: University of Florida IFAS Extension — Crotalaria spectabilis for Nematode Management
USDA SARE — Cover Crop Encyclopedia: Crotalaria
USDA APHIS — Crotalaria spectabilis Toxicology Alert
